records.name: FTC Act Section 5 — Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices (website deception)

citation.section: Section 5, 15 U.S.C. §45(a); enforcement/penalty authority §5(l), §5(m); .com Disclosures guidance (2013)

applies_when: markets any product or service to US consumers through the website
makes objective claims (performance, price, savings, results, 'free', comparisons) a reasonable consumer would rely on
excluded_when: purely non-commercial content with no offer
puffery a reasonable consumer would not read as a factual claim

website_obligations.duty: Do not publish false, misleading or deceptive representations about a product or service (the breach is a deceptive claim being present on the site)
elements: no false or unsupported performance, health, earnings or savings claims
no deceptive omission of a fact material to a purchase decision

website_obligations.duty: Hold a reasonable basis (competent and reliable evidence) for every objective claim before it is published
elements: reasonable basis (competent and reliable evidence) held for objective claims at time of publication
substantiation available for performance, health, earnings or savings claims

website_obligations.duty: Material terms and disclosures are clear and conspicuous, not buried in fine print, hyperlinks, or hover text (.com Disclosures)
elements: disclosures near the triggering claim and unavoidable before purchase
total price and material conditions ('free' offers, auto-charges, restrictions) disclosed up front

website_obligations.duty: No dark patterns that trick or trap the consumer (obstructed cancellation, pre-checked add-ons, false urgency, hidden fees)
elements: no fake countdown timers or false scarcity
no pre-selected paid extras presented as consent
symmetry between how a consumer signs up and how they cancel

penalty.basis: no per se fine for a first §5(a) violation; civil penalties up to $53,088 per violation (2025 adjustment) attach to conduct that violates an FTC rule or a prior order, plus redress/disgorgement; typical band reflects negotiated monetary judgments

regulator.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC); state attorneys general concurrently under state UDAP

enforcement.summary: First FTC action over suppression of negative reviews; presenting a curated 4-5 star wall as all reviews was a deceptive §5 practice.

intel.why_matters: Section 5 is the backstop the FTC reaches for when no specific rule fits; almost every misleading headline, fake 'was/now' price, unsupported 'clinically proven' or 'guaranteed results' line on a US site is a §5 exposure.
intel.regulator_asks_first: What is the net impression to a reasonable consumer, and what evidence did you hold before you published the claim?
intel.relevance_hook: The deception standard is impression-based, so a technically-true-but-misleading page still breaches; this is the single most common finding on a US commercial site.

records.name: FTC Endorsement Guides 2023 + Consumer Reviews and Testimonials Rule (16 CFR Part 465)

citation.act: FTC Act §5; Guides Concerning the Use of Endorsements and Testimonials (16 CFR Part 255, revised 2023); Trade Regulation Rule on the Use of Consumer Reviews and Testimonials (16 CFR Part 465, 2024)
citation.section: 16 CFR Part 255 (effective 26 Jun 2023, 88 FR 48092); 16 CFR Part 465 (effective 21 Oct 2024)

applies_when: displays reviews, testimonials, ratings, star counts or endorsements
uses influencers, affiliates, brand ambassadors or gifted/paid creators
publishes any endorsement by an employee, officer or related party
excluded_when: no reviews, testimonials or endorsements anywhere on the site or linked social
reviews are genuine, unfiltered, and carry no material connection

website_obligations.duty: Clearly and conspicuously disclose every material connection between an endorser and the brand, near the claim
elements: paid, gifted, employee, affiliate or equity relationships disclosed
disclosure proximate to the endorsement, not only in a bio, hashtag stack, or 'see more'
affiliate recommendation pages carry a plain-language disclosure

website_obligations.duty: Do not publish, buy, sell or incentivise fake, AI-generated, or misrepresented reviews (Part 465 binding prohibitions)
elements: no fabricated reviews or reviews from people with no real experience
no review suppression or presenting a filtered subset as representative
no compensation conditioned on a positive or particular-sentiment review
insider (officer/manager/employee) reviews disclose the connection

website_obligations.duty: Do not misrepresent testimonial results; disclose when depicted results are not typical
elements: 'results not typical' or generally-expected-results disclosure where atypical outcomes are shown

penalty.basis: Part 465 is a Trade Regulation Rule, so knowing violations carry civil penalties up to $53,088 per violation (2025 adjustment) plus consumer redress; the Guides themselves are interpretive but breaches are actionable as §5 deception

enforcement.case: FTC v. Fashion Nova, LLC (review suppression)
enforcement.summary: Blocking sub-four-star reviews and displaying only high ratings misrepresented the body of reviews; foundational case now codified into Part 465.

intel.why_matters: Since 21 Oct 2024 fake and suppressed reviews are not just deception, they carry per-violation civil penalties under a binding rule; undisclosed influencer and affiliate relationships remain the most-cited endorsement failure.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are all reviews genuine and unfiltered, and is every paid or gifted relationship disclosed where the consumer sees the claim?
intel.relevance_hook: Star widgets, testimonial carousels and affiliate 'we recommend' pages are exactly what an auditor screens; a missing #ad or a curated review wall is now a penalty-grade finding.

records.name: CAN-SPAM Act — commercial email requirements

citation.act: Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography And Marketing Act of 2003
citation.section: 15 U.S.C. §§7701-7713; FTC CAN-SPAM Rule, 16 CFR Part 316

applies_when: sends commercial email (primary purpose is advertising or promoting a product or service)
captures email addresses through the site for marketing
excluded_when: transactional or relationship messages only (order confirmations, receipts, account notices)

website_obligations.duty: Every commercial email must carry accurate routing and a valid physical postal address
elements: truthful 'From', 'To' and header/routing information
non-deceptive subject line
valid physical postal address (street address, PO box, or registered agent) in the footer

website_obligations.duty: Provide a clear opt-out and honour it (opt-out model, not opt-in)
elements: conspicuous unsubscribe mechanism in every commercial email
opt-out honoured within 10 business days and kept working for at least 30 days
no fee or extra information demanded to unsubscribe

penalty.basis: civil penalties up to $53,088 per non-compliant email (2025 adjustment); aggregate judgments reach the millions where volume is high

regulator.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC); also enforceable by state AGs and ISPs

intel.why_matters: CAN-SPAM is a per-email liability; a newsletter footer missing a postal address or a broken unsubscribe multiplies fast across a send list.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does every marketing email carry a real postal address and a one-click unsubscribe you honour within ten business days?
intel.relevance_hook: Auditors test the signup-to-confirmation flow; a missing physical address in the email footer is a black-and-white presence failure the client can fix same day.

records.name: Telephone Consumer Protection Act — web consent capture for calls and texts

citation.section: 47 U.S.C. §227; FCC rules 47 CFR §64.1200; revocation-of-consent rule effective 11 Apr 2025

applies_when: collects a phone number through a web form for marketing calls or SMS
sends autodialed or prerecorded marketing calls or texts to US numbers
excluded_when: no marketing calls or texts (purely transactional or manually dialled non-marketing contact)
number captured solely for a one-off transactional message the consumer requested

website_obligations.duty: Obtain prior express written consent for autodialed/prerecorded marketing calls and texts at the point of capture
elements: unchecked, affirmative consent tied to the specific seller
clear disclosure that consent is not a condition of purchase
consent language and the phone field visible together at submit

website_obligations.duty: Provide and honour a simple revocation ('STOP' in any reasonable manner), honoured within 10 business days
elements: STOP/UNSUBSCRIBE opt-out accepted by any reasonable method
at most one confirmation message, carrying no marketing content

penalty.basis: private right of action: $500 per negligent violation, up to $1,500 per willful violation, per call or text; class exposure runs to seven and eight figures

regulator.name: Federal Communications Commission (FCC); private right of action and state AGs

intel.why_matters: TCPA is plaintiff-lawyer territory: each text is its own statutory-damages unit, so a lead form that captures a phone number without proper consent is a class-action magnet.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Where is the express-written-consent language, is it unchecked, and does it say consent is not required to buy?
intel.relevance_hook: The FCC one-to-one consent rule was vacated (11th Cir, Jan 2025) so bundled consent is again permissible, but the core express-written-consent and STOP duties are unchanged and easy to verify on any phone-capture form.

records.name: ADA Title III — website accessibility for places of public accommodation

citation.act: Americans with Disabilities Act, Title III
citation.section: 42 U.S.C. §12181 et seq.; 28 CFR Part 36; DOJ web accessibility guidance (2022, references WCAG)

applies_when: operates a website connected to a business the courts treat as a place of public accommodation (retail, hospitality, services, healthcare, food, professional offices)
the site is the gateway to goods or services offered to the US public
excluded_when: purely internal or B2B-only tools with no public accommodation nexus
in the Third, Fifth, Sixth, Ninth and Eleventh Circuits, a standalone web-only business with no physical place may fall outside Title III (a genuine circuit split, not settled law)

website_obligations.duty: Provide a website accessible to people with disabilities; courts and DOJ measure conformance against WCAG (2.0/2.1 Level AA)
elements: text alternatives for images and non-text content
programmatic labels on forms and controls
keyboard operability with no keyboard traps
sufficient colour contrast
captions for video, and accessible error messaging

website_obligations.duty: Offer an accessible alternative contact route where full conformance is not yet achieved
elements: accessibility statement with a monitored contact for access requests

penalty.basis: no fixed statutory web penalty; most exposure is private-lawsuit settlement plus plaintiff attorneys' fees (commonly five figures); DOJ civil penalties apply in government-initiated Title III actions

regulator.name: US Department of Justice (DOJ); overwhelmingly enforced via private ADA lawsuits

intel.why_matters: Website accessibility suits and demand letters number in the thousands each year; they target obvious WCAG failures (missing alt text, unlabeled forms, poor contrast) that a scan surfaces immediately.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Can a screen-reader and keyboard-only user complete the same core tasks (browse, add to cart, check out, contact you) as everyone else?
intel.relevance_hook: Honestly flagged: whether Title III reaches a web-only business is circuit-split, but for any business with a physical location the accessibility duty is live and the defects are cheap to detect and fix.

records.name: Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) — under-13 data collection

citation.section: 15 U.S.C. §§6501-6506; FTC COPPA Rule 16 CFR Part 312 (amended final rule effective 23 Jun 2025, compliance by 22 Apr 2026)

applies_when: the site or service is directed to children under 13, or
the operator has actual knowledge it collects personal information from a child under 13
excluded_when: not child-directed and no actual knowledge of under-13 users
audience is general or the content is clearly directed to teens or adults (subject to the multi-factor child-directed test)

website_obligations.duty: Post a clear COPPA privacy notice and obtain verifiable parental consent before collecting a child's personal information
elements: child-specific privacy policy describing what is collected and how it is used
verifiable parental consent obtained before collection (now including facial-recognition-with-review, KBA, and other approved methods)
separate consent for disclosure to third parties unless integral to the service
direct notice to parents

website_obligations.duty: Minimise collection, honour parental rights, and limit tracking on child-directed pages
elements: collect only what is reasonably necessary
parental access and deletion route provided
written data-retention policy posted; no indefinite retention
no behavioural-advertising cookies/SDKs firing on child-directed pages without consent

penalty.basis: civil penalties up to $53,088 per violation (per child); aggregate judgments have reached $275m (Epic Games); the statutory_max is the per-violation cap, the high figure is the largest actual penalty

enforcement.summary: Largest COPPA penalty ever: Fortnite collected under-13 data and enabled voice/text chat by default without parental notice or consent.

intel.why_matters: COPPA is a bright-line gate: if a site is child-directed or knowingly collects under-13 data, the parental-consent and notice duties are absolute, and the penalties are per-child.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is this service child-directed, and if so where is the verifiable parental consent before any data is collected?
intel.relevance_hook: The 2025 Rule amendments (effective Jun 2025, full compliance Apr 2026) add a data-retention-policy duty and new consent methods, so even previously compliant child-directed sites need a refresh.

records.name: Restore Online Shoppers' Confidence Act (ROSCA) — online subscriptions and auto-renewal

citation.act: Restore Online Shoppers' Confidence Act; FTC Negative Option Rule (pre-2024 prenotification version, 16 CFR Part 425)
citation.section: 15 U.S.C. §§8401-8405 (esp. §8403); Negative Option Rule 16 CFR Part 425

applies_when: sells any subscription, membership, free-trial-to-paid, or auto-renewing plan online
charges on a negative-option basis (silence or inaction is treated as acceptance)
excluded_when: no recurring charge or auto-renewal; one-time purchases only

website_obligations.duty: Clearly disclose all material terms before obtaining billing information
elements: price, billing frequency, renewal, and any trial-to-paid conversion disclosed near the payment step
disclosure clear and conspicuous, not buried in terms of service

website_obligations.duty: Obtain express informed consent to the negative-option feature before charging
elements: affirmative consent specific to the recurring charge, separate from the general purchase

website_obligations.duty: Provide a simple mechanism to cancel and stop future charges
elements: a cancellation path that exists and is not obstructed
no forced phone-only cancellation where signup was online

penalty.basis: ROSCA violations are treated as violations of an FTC rule, so civil penalties up to $53,088 per violation (2025 adjustment) plus redress apply; state Automatic Renewal Laws add parallel penalties

regulator.name: Federal Trade Commission (FTC); state AGs under state Automatic Renewal Laws

intel.why_matters: Subscriptions are a top FTC and state-AG enforcement priority; the exposure sits in unclear renewal disclosure, pre-checked auto-renewal, and cancellation friction.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Before you took the card, did you clearly disclose the recurring charge, get separate consent to it, and is cancellation as easy as signup?
intel.relevance_hook: VERIFIED status: the FTC 2024 'Click-to-Cancel' four-step Negative Option Rule was vacated in full by the Eighth Circuit on 8 Jul 2025 (Custom Communications v. FTC), so the pre-2024 Negative Option Rule, ROSCA, and state ARLs govern; do not audit against click-to-cancel steps. The FTC submitted a new ANPRM in Jan 2026, so watch for a replacement rule.

records.name: FTC Green Guides — environmental marketing claims (16 CFR Part 260)

citation.act: FTC Act §5; Guides for the Use of Environmental Marketing Claims

applies_when: makes environmental or sustainability claims (eco-friendly, green, recyclable, compostable, carbon-neutral, non-toxic, sustainable)
excluded_when: no environmental marketing claims on the site

website_obligations.duty: Every environmental claim is truthful, substantiated, specific, and qualified so it is not deceptive
elements: no unqualified general benefit claims ('eco-friendly', 'green') without close, prominent qualification
'recyclable' only where recycling facilities are available to a substantial majority of consumers/communities
carbon-neutral/offset claims disclose the project, standard, and timing
certification and seal claims name the certifying body and the specific basis

penalty.basis: the Guides are interpretive; deceptive green claims are enforced as §5 violations, and where tied to a rule or order carry civil penalties up to $53,088 per violation plus corrective advertising

intel.why_matters: Greenwashing is a stated FTC priority and the Green Guides are the yardstick; vague 'sustainable' or 'carbon-neutral' badges without qualification are the classic finding.
intel.regulator_asks_first: What specific, substantiated environmental benefit are you claiming, and can a consumer see the qualification and evidence next to the claim?
intel.relevance_hook: The Green Guides are under FTC review, but the deception standard they codify is live now; any 'eco' badge or recyclability claim on a US product page is auditable against them today.

records.name: FTC Made in USA Labeling Rule — origin claims (16 CFR Part 323)

citation.act: FTC Made in USA Labeling Rule; FTC Act §5

applies_when: makes unqualified 'Made in USA', 'Made in America', or 'Assembled in USA' claims on product pages or marketing
excluded_when: no US-origin claims made

website_obligations.duty: Do not make an unqualified US-origin claim unless the product is 'all or virtually all' made in the United States
elements: final assembly or processing in the US
all significant processing in the US
all or virtually all ingredients or components made and sourced in the US
qualified claims ('Made in USA from imported parts') accurate where used

penalty.basis: the Labeling Rule is a Trade Regulation Rule; violations carry civil penalties up to $53,088 per violation (2025 adjustment) plus redress

intel.why_matters: Since 2021 the FTC has a dedicated rule with per-violation civil penalties for false US-origin claims; 'Made in USA' badges are a specific, checkable claim with real teeth.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is this product 'all or virtually all' made in the US, and if not, is the origin claim properly qualified?
intel.relevance_hook: A single 'Made in USA' banner on an imported or mixed-origin product is a discrete, penalty-grade finding that resonates strongly with US SMBs.

records.name: California Consumer Privacy Act, as amended by the CPRA

citation.act: California Consumer Privacy Act, as amended by the CPRA
citation.section: Cal. Civ. Code §1798.100 et seq.; CPPA regulations (cyber audit / risk assessment / ADMT rules effective 1 Jan 2026)

applies_when: processes personal data of CA residents AND meets the state applicability threshold
advisory: attach only where an observable nexus to state residents exists; do not assert as a breach without confirming the business meets the threshold
excluded_when: does not apply below all CCPA thresholds: (a) annual gross revenue over $26,625,000 (2026 inflation-adjusted from $25m); (b) buys/sells/shares personal data of 100,000+ CA consumers or households; or (c) derives 50%+ of revenue from selling/sharing personal data
many state acts exempt data/entities already covered by GLBA, HIPAA, FCRA, or non-profits (varies by state)

website_obligations.duty: Publish a CCPA privacy policy and the required opt-out links
elements: categories of personal information collected, sold, or shared and purposes
'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link
'Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information' link where sensitive PI is used
consumer rights (know, delete, correct, opt-out) and how to exercise them

website_obligations.duty: Honour the Global Privacy Control browser signal as a valid opt-out of sale/sharing
elements: GPC signal treated as an opt-out request without further action
opt-out applied to cookie-based ad/analytics 'sales'

penalty.basis: $2,500 per unintentional violation and $7,500 per intentional violation or violation involving a minor; enforced by the CA AG and the California Privacy Protection Agency

regulator.name: CA Attorney General and California Privacy Protection Agency

enforcement.case: California v. Sephora USA, Inc. (AG settlement)
enforcement.summary: First public CCPA enforcement: failure to disclose data 'sales', provide a Do-Not-Sell link, and honour Global Privacy Control signals.

intel.why_matters: A CA-resident nexus plus threshold means the site needs CA-specific privacy disclosures and opt-out plumbing; the visible tells (privacy link, opt-out link, GPC honouring) are auditable.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does your privacy notice cover our residents' rights, and do you honour opt-out and universal opt-out signals?
intel.relevance_hook: Advisory tier: flag as an obligation to confirm, not a hard breach, unless the business plainly meets the threshold; the fix (policy + opt-out link + GPC) is the same baseline across states.

records.name: Virginia Consumer Data Protection Act
applies_when: processes personal data of VA residents AND meets the state applicability threshold
advisory: attach only where an observable nexus to state residents exists; do not assert as a breach without confirming the business meets the threshold
excluded_when: does not apply below thresholds: controls/processes personal data of 100,000+ VA consumers, OR 25,000+ VA consumers while deriving over 50% of gross revenue from the sale of personal data
many state acts exempt data/entities already covered by GLBA, HIPAA, FCRA, or non-profits (varies by state)

website_obligations.duty: Publish a compliant privacy notice describing categories, purposes, sharing, and consumer rights
elements: categories of personal data collected and purposes
how to exercise access, correction, deletion, and portability rights
third-party sharing/sale/targeted-advertising disclosures

website_obligations.duty: Offer the opt-outs the state requires and honour universal opt-out signals where mandated
elements: opt-out of sale and of targeted advertising
recognise the Global Privacy Control / universal opt-out signal where the state requires it
opt-in consent for sensitive data where required

penalty.basis: civil penalties typically up to $7,500 per violation, enforced by the state AG (California adds the CPPA); most states provide a cure period

intel.why_matters: A VA-resident nexus plus threshold means the site needs VA-specific privacy disclosures and opt-out plumbing; the visible tells (privacy link, opt-out link, GPC honouring) are auditable.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does your privacy notice cover our residents' rights, and do you honour opt-out and universal opt-out signals?
intel.relevance_hook: Advisory tier: flag as an obligation to confirm, not a hard breach, unless the business plainly meets the threshold; the fix (policy + opt-out link + GPC) is the same baseline across states.

records.name: Colorado Privacy Act

citation.section: Colo. Rev. Stat. §6-1-1301 et seq.; Colorado AG rules (universal opt-out mechanism required)

applies_when: processes personal data of CO residents AND meets the state applicability threshold
advisory: attach only where an observable nexus to state residents exists; do not assert as a breach without confirming the business meets the threshold
excluded_when: does not apply below thresholds: controls/processes personal data of 100,000+ CO consumers, OR 25,000+ CO consumers while deriving revenue or receiving a discount from selling personal data
many state acts exempt data/entities already covered by GLBA, HIPAA, FCRA, or non-profits (varies by state)

website_obligations.duty: Publish a compliant privacy notice describing categories, purposes, sharing, and consumer rights
elements: categories of personal data collected and purposes
how to exercise access, correction, deletion, and portability rights
third-party sharing/sale/targeted-advertising disclosures

website_obligations.duty: Offer the opt-outs the state requires and honour universal opt-out signals where mandated
elements: opt-out of sale and of targeted advertising
recognise the Global Privacy Control / universal opt-out signal where the state requires it
opt-in consent for sensitive data where required

penalty.basis: Colorado Privacy Act violations are deceptive trade practices under the Colorado Consumer Protection Act, C.R.S. 6-1-112: civil penalties up to 20,000 dollars per violation (each consumer or transaction a separate violation), enforced by the Colorado Attorney General and district attorneys; the right-to-cure period closed 1 January 2025

intel.why_matters: A CO-resident nexus plus threshold means the site needs CO-specific privacy disclosures and opt-out plumbing; the visible tells (privacy link, opt-out link, GPC honouring) are auditable.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does your privacy notice cover our residents' rights, and do you honour opt-out and universal opt-out signals?
intel.relevance_hook: Advisory tier: flag as an obligation to confirm, not a hard breach, unless the business plainly meets the threshold; the fix (policy + opt-out link + GPC) is the same baseline across states.

sources: Colorado Revised Statutes 6-1-112 (Colorado Consumer Protection Act civil penalties)

records.name: Connecticut Data Privacy Act

citation.section: Conn. Gen. Stat. §42-515 et seq. (Public Act 22-15)

applies_when: processes personal data of CT residents AND meets the state applicability threshold
advisory: attach only where an observable nexus to state residents exists; do not assert as a breach without confirming the business meets the threshold
excluded_when: does not apply below thresholds: controls/processes personal data of 100,000+ CT consumers (excluding payment-only data), OR 25,000+ CT consumers while deriving over 25% of gross revenue from selling personal data
many state acts exempt data/entities already covered by GLBA, HIPAA, FCRA, or non-profits (varies by state)

website_obligations.duty: Publish a compliant privacy notice describing categories, purposes, sharing, and consumer rights
elements: categories of personal data collected and purposes
how to exercise access, correction, deletion, and portability rights
third-party sharing/sale/targeted-advertising disclosures

website_obligations.duty: Offer the opt-outs the state requires and honour universal opt-out signals where mandated
elements: opt-out of sale and of targeted advertising
recognise the Global Privacy Control / universal opt-out signal where the state requires it
opt-in consent for sensitive data where required

penalty.basis: civil penalties typically up to $7,500 per violation, enforced by the state AG (California adds the CPPA); most states provide a cure period

intel.why_matters: A CT-resident nexus plus threshold means the site needs CT-specific privacy disclosures and opt-out plumbing; the visible tells (privacy link, opt-out link, GPC honouring) are auditable.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does your privacy notice cover our residents' rights, and do you honour opt-out and universal opt-out signals?
intel.relevance_hook: Advisory tier: flag as an obligation to confirm, not a hard breach, unless the business plainly meets the threshold; the fix (policy + opt-out link + GPC) is the same baseline across states.

records.name: Utah Consumer Privacy Act
applies_when: processes personal data of UT residents AND meets the state applicability threshold
advisory: attach only where an observable nexus to state residents exists; do not assert as a breach without confirming the business meets the threshold
excluded_when: does not apply unless annual revenue is $25,000,000+ AND (controls/processes data of 100,000+ UT consumers, OR 25,000+ UT consumers while deriving over 50% of revenue from selling personal data); Utah is the most business-favourable and has no universal-opt-out mandate
many state acts exempt data/entities already covered by GLBA, HIPAA, FCRA, or non-profits (varies by state)

website_obligations.duty: Publish a compliant privacy notice describing categories, purposes, sharing, and consumer rights
elements: categories of personal data collected and purposes
how to exercise access, correction, deletion, and portability rights
third-party sharing/sale/targeted-advertising disclosures

website_obligations.duty: Offer the opt-outs the state requires and honour universal opt-out signals where mandated
elements: opt-out of sale and of targeted advertising
recognise the Global Privacy Control / universal opt-out signal where the state requires it
opt-in consent for sensitive data where required

penalty.basis: civil penalties typically up to $7,500 per violation, enforced by the state AG (California adds the CPPA); most states provide a cure period

intel.why_matters: A UT-resident nexus plus threshold means the site needs UT-specific privacy disclosures and opt-out plumbing; the visible tells (privacy link, opt-out link, GPC honouring) are auditable.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does your privacy notice cover our residents' rights, and do you honour opt-out and universal opt-out signals?
intel.relevance_hook: Advisory tier: flag as an obligation to confirm, not a hard breach, unless the business plainly meets the threshold; the fix (policy + opt-out link + GPC) is the same baseline across states.

records.name: Texas Data Privacy and Security Act

citation.act: Texas Data Privacy and Security Act

applies_when: processes personal data of TX residents AND meets the state applicability threshold
advisory: attach only where an observable nexus to state residents exists; do not assert as a breach without confirming the business meets the threshold
excluded_when: no headcount threshold: applies to any business that processes/sells personal data and is NOT a small business (as defined by the US SBA), unless it only sells data with prior consent; small-business exemption is the key gate
many state acts exempt data/entities already covered by GLBA, HIPAA, FCRA, or non-profits (varies by state)

website_obligations.duty: Publish a compliant privacy notice describing categories, purposes, sharing, and consumer rights
elements: categories of personal data collected and purposes
how to exercise access, correction, deletion, and portability rights
third-party sharing/sale/targeted-advertising disclosures

website_obligations.duty: Offer the opt-outs the state requires and honour universal opt-out signals where mandated
elements: opt-out of sale and of targeted advertising
recognise the Global Privacy Control / universal opt-out signal where the state requires it
opt-in consent for sensitive data where required

penalty.basis: civil penalties typically up to $7,500 per violation, enforced by the state AG (California adds the CPPA); most states provide a cure period

intel.why_matters: A TX-resident nexus plus threshold means the site needs TX-specific privacy disclosures and opt-out plumbing; the visible tells (privacy link, opt-out link, GPC honouring) are auditable.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does your privacy notice cover our residents' rights, and do you honour opt-out and universal opt-out signals?
intel.relevance_hook: Advisory tier: flag as an obligation to confirm, not a hard breach, unless the business plainly meets the threshold; the fix (policy + opt-out link + GPC) is the same baseline across states.

records.name: US comprehensive state privacy laws — 2024-2026 wave (advisory overlay)
records.qa_note: aggregate multi-state overview cannot carry one official citation; excluded from artifact until split into per-state records with official citations (P5 law wave)

citation.act: State comprehensive consumer privacy acts effective 2024-2026 (OR, MT, DE, IA, NE, NH, NJ, TN, MN, MD, IN, and later RI/KY/IN)
citation.section: e.g. Oregon OCPA (1 Jul 2024); Montana MCDPA (1 Oct 2024); Delaware DPDPA, Iowa ICDPA, Nebraska NDPA, New Hampshire NHDPL (1 Jan 2025); New Jersey NJDPA (15 Jan 2025); Tennessee TIPA (1 Jul 2025); Minnesota MCDPA (31 Jul 2025); Maryland MODPA (1 Oct 2025); Indiana INCDPA (1 Jan 2026)

applies_when: processes personal data of residents of any state in the 2024-2026 wave AND meets that state's threshold
advisory: treat as a portfolio of near-identical baselines; attach per-state only on observable resident nexus and confirmed threshold
excluded_when: below each state's applicability threshold (most mirror the 100,000-consumer / 25,000-plus-data-sales model; Nebraska and Texas use an SBA small-business gate; thresholds vary)
GLBA/HIPAA/FCRA-covered data and non-profits are commonly exempt (varies by state)

website_obligations.duty: Maintain one baseline privacy programme that satisfies the common denominator of these acts
elements: comprehensive privacy notice with categories, purposes, sharing, and rights
opt-out of sale and targeted advertising, plus opt-in for sensitive data
recognise universal opt-out / Global Privacy Control where the state requires it (OR, MT, DE, NJ, MN, MD and others)
data protection assessments for high-risk processing (internal, but referenced in the notice)

penalty.basis: per-violation civil penalties typically up to $7,500, enforced by each state AG; Maryland notably bans the sale of sensitive data outright

intel.why_matters: By 2026 roughly twenty states have comprehensive privacy laws with a shared shape; a single well-built privacy baseline covers most, so the finding is usually 'you meet a threshold somewhere and lack the baseline', not a state-by-state audit.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Do you have a privacy notice and opt-out mechanism that meets the multistate baseline, and do you honour universal opt-out signals?
intel.relevance_hook: Advisory tier: this record exists so the engine can note multistate exposure without asserting twenty separate breaches; the remediation is one baseline, not twenty.

records.name: Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act (BIPA)

citation.section: 740 ILCS 14/1 et seq. (esp. §15 notice/consent, §20 damages; SB 2979 single-violation amendment effective 2 Aug 2024)

applies_when: collects biometric identifiers or information from Illinois residents (face/voice/fingerprint geometry, retina, hand scans)
uses website or app features such as facial recognition, face filters, voiceprints, or fingerprint/face login
excluded_when: no biometric data collected from Illinois residents
financial institutions covered by GLBA and certain health data are excluded

website_obligations.duty: Publish a public written biometric data retention and destruction policy
elements: retention schedule and destruction guidelines publicly available

website_obligations.duty: Obtain informed written consent before collecting biometric data
elements: written notice of the specific purpose and length of collection/storage/use before capture
written release obtained (electronic signature permitted since the 2024 amendment)
no sale or profit from biometric data; disclosure only with consent

penalty.basis: private right of action with liquidated damages of $1,000 (negligent) or $5,000 (reckless/intentional) per violation plus fees; since SB 2979 (Aug 2024) repeated same-method collection is a single violation, but class exposure remains substantial

regulator.name: No state agency: enforced through a private right of action in Illinois courts

intel.why_matters: BIPA is the most litigated US biometric statute, with a private right of action and liquidated damages per violation; any face filter, voiceprint, or biometric login on a site reaching Illinois residents is exposure.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Do you have a public biometric retention policy and written informed consent obtained before any biometric capture?
intel.relevance_hook: Advisory tier (Illinois only), but high-value: the two visible tells (a public retention policy and a pre-capture consent step) are easy to check and the damages model is what makes BIPA feared.

records.name: State UDAP / 'mini-FTC' acts — unfair and deceptive practices (advisory overlay)

citation.act: State unfair and deceptive acts and practices (UDAP) statutes; every state has one (e.g., California UCL/FAL, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §§17200 & 17500; Florida FDUTPA; Massachusetts Ch. 93A; New York GBL §349)
citation.section: Representative anchor: Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §17200 (unfair competition) and §17500 (false advertising)

applies_when: markets or sells to consumers in any US state (every state has a UDAP statute paralleling FTC §5)
makes a deceptive or unfair representation reachable by that state's residents
excluded_when: purely non-commercial content; some states' UDAP acts exclude regulated activities (e.g., insurance, securities) covered elsewhere

website_obligations.duty: The same truthful, substantiated, non-deceptive standard as FTC §5 applies at state level, often with a private right of action and, in some states, statutory or treble damages
elements: no deceptive pricing, false 'sale' anchors, or misleading claims
material terms disclosed clearly
no unfair practices (undue pressure, exploitation, hidden material terms)

penalty.basis: per-violation penalties vary widely by state (commonly $2,000-$10,000 per violation); many acts add a private right of action, attorneys' fees, and in some states treble or statutory damages

regulator.name: State Attorneys General; frequently a private right of action for consumers

intel.why_matters: Even where the FTC does not act, a deceptive US page is exposed under all fifty states' UDAP laws, several of which let consumers sue directly and recover multiplied damages.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is the claim truthful and substantiated to the same standard a state AG or a consumer plaintiff would apply?
intel.relevance_hook: Advisory single record: it tells the client that FTC §5 findings also carry state-law and private-litigation exposure, without generating fifty near-identical rows.
